description: Mater Amabilis Year 5 living books curriculum for grade 5. The Plutarch rotation continues, American history moves from the colonies toward the founding era, and written narration becomes the primary mode across most subjects.

books.title: Adam of the Road
books.author: Elizabeth Janet Gray
books.subject: living_books
books.notes: A minstrel boy's travels across medieval England searching for his lost dog and father. Newbery Medal winner. 'What does Adam learn about home along the way?'

books.title: The Door in the Wall
books.author: Marguerite de Angeli
books.subject: living_books
books.notes: A boy who cannot walk finds a different kind of usefulness and courage. 'What was the door in the wall, really?'

books.title: The Voyage of the Dawn Treader
books.author: C. S. Lewis
books.subject: living_books
books.notes: Each island tests a different temptation — greed, vanity, despair. Pairs well with this year's growing emphasis on written narration of character change. 'What changed Eustace from the inside, not just the outside?'

books.title: Plutarch's Lives (selected passages, continuing rotation)
books.author: Plutarch
books.subject: history
books.notes: Continuing the multi-year rotation begun in Year 3. Read slowly, one incident at a time. 'Was this choice virtuous, or only effective?'

books.title: The Story of the Thirteen Colonies
books.author: H. A. Guerber
books.subject: history
books.notes: Guerber's narrative history moves from early settlement toward the founding era, picking up where Year 4's George Washington's World left off. 'What made each colony's founding different from the others?'

books.title: Trial and Triumph: Stories from Church History (continuing)
books.author: Richard Hannula
books.subject: saints
books.notes: Continuing the rotation. 'What did this person risk, and why did they think it was worth it?'

books.title: Madam How and Lady Why (second half)
books.author: Charles Kingsley
books.subject: science
books.notes: Continuing from Year 4's first half. Read a chapter, then look for a real example outdoors if possible. 'What question would you ask Madam How this time?'

books.title: Archimedes and the Door of Science
books.author: Jeanne Bendick
books.subject: science
books.notes: A short, lively biography of Archimedes and the birth of scientific reasoning in the ancient world. Ties naturally to this term's Plutarch reading on ancient Greece and Rome.

books.title: Handbook of Nature Study (continuing reference)
books.author: Anna Botsford Comstock
books.subject: nature_study
books.notes: A reference, not a read-aloud — consult for whatever the child has just found outside. Pair every entry with a nature journal sketch.

subject_plans.mathematics: Grade 5 scope (Common Core domains 5.OA, 5.NBT, 5.NF, 5.MD, 5.G): decimal place value and operations with decimals to hundredths; adding and subtracting fractions with unlike denominators, and multiplying/dividing fractions; volume of rectangular prisms; plotting points on a coordinate plane. Mater Amabilis approach: real-world problems (recipes, measurements, maps) before the abstract notation.
subject_plans.art_music: Composer: Franz Schubert — lyrical, singable melodies (try 'The Trout' quintet), a warm middle ground between Classical clarity and Romantic feeling. Artist: Albrecht Dürer — precise, richly detailed engravings and woodcuts that reward slow, close looking. Ask what the child notices only on a second look. Poet: Alfred, Lord Tennyson — narrative and historical poems with strong rhythm (The Charge of the Light Brigade), pairing naturally with this year's history reading.
subject_plans.language_arts: Grade 5: written narration is now the primary mode for most subjects, not the exception. Dictation passages grow longer and more complex. Grammar covers more sophisticated sentence structures (compound and complex sentences), and the student begins a simple outline before narrating a longer passage.
subject_plans.morning_time: Scripture with more substantive discussion of its meaning, a hymn or folk song, and Tennyson for poetry. Connect briefly to this term's history rotation — the colonies, or Adam of the Road's medieval England.
